---
title: "Chapter 1: Comparison of Batches"
subtitle: "Applied Multivariate Statistical Analysis"
author: "Härdle & Simar"
date: today
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    fonttheme: structurebold
    aspectratio: 169
    navigation: horizontal
    section-titles: true
    toc: false
    toc-depth: 1
    toc-title: "Outline"
    slide-level: 2
    header-includes: |
      \usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,bm}
      \usepackage{booktabs}
      \usepackage{graphicx}
      \usepackage{xcolor}
      \definecolor{mpiblue}{RGB}{0,90,169}
      \setbeamercolor{frametitle}{bg=mpiblue}
      \setbeamercolor{progress bar}{fg=mpiblue}
      \newcommand{\indc}{\mathbf{1}}
      \DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}
---

# Introduction

## What Is Multivariate Statistical Analysis?

**Setting:** We observe $n$ data points in $\mathbb{R}^p$:
$$
x_i = (x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \dots, x_{ip}), \quad i = 1, \dots, n,
$$
each a realisation of a random vector $X = (X_1, X_2, \dots, X_p)^\top \in \mathbb{R}^p$.

**Key questions addressed by descriptive techniques:**

- Are some components more spread than others?
- Do sub-groups of observations exist?
- Are there outliers?
- How "normal" is the distribution?
- Are there low-dimensional linear combinations of $X$ with non-normal behaviour?

## Perceptual Challenges in High Dimensions

- **2D:** Point clouds are immediately interpretable.
- **3D:** Real-time rotation on a computer screen is feasible.
- **4D:** A "sliding" technique (Härdle \& Scott, 1992) can show 3D density contours as the fourth variable varies.
- **$\geqslant\;$5D:** A qualitative jump in difficulty occurs; high-dimensional structure must be mapped into lower dimensions (Klinke \& Polzehl, 1995).

**This chapter** introduces:

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Boxplots
- Histograms
- Kernel density estimates
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Scatterplots \& draftman plots
- Chernoff–Flury faces
- Andrews' curves
- Parallel coordinates plots (PCP)
:::
:::

## The Swiss Bank Note Dataset

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Swiss bank notes** (Flury \& Riedwyl, 1988): $n = 200$ measurements — 100 **genuine**, 100 **counterfeit**.

Six measurements per note:
\begin{align*}
X_1 &= \text{length of bill}\\
X_2 &= \text{height (left)}\\
X_3 &= \text{height (right)}\\
X_4 &= \text{inner frame to lower border}\\
X_5 &= \text{inner frame to upper border}\\
X_6 &= \text{diagonal of central picture}
\end{align*}

**Goal:** Use these measurements to determine whether a bill is genuine or counterfeit.
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
```{=latex}
\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[page=1,width=\linewidth,height=0.62\textheight,keepaspectratio]%
    {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
  \caption{Swiss 1000-franc bank note with measurement locations}
\end{figure}
```
:::
::::::::::::::

# Section 1: Boxplots

## The Five Number Summary

**Example:** 15 largest world cities in 2006 (population in 10,000s).

\begin{table}
\centering\footnotesize
\begin{tabular}{llrr}
\toprule
City & Country & Pop.\ (10k) & Order stat.\\
\midrule
Tokyo & Japan & 3{,}420 & $x_{(15)}$\\
Mexico City & Mexico & 2{,}280 & $x_{(14)}$\\
Seoul & S.\ Korea & 2{,}230 & $x_{(13)}$\\
New York & USA & 2{,}190 & $x_{(12)}$\\
Sao Paulo & Brazil & 2{,}020 & $x_{(11)}$\\
Bombay & India & 1{,}985 & $x_{(10)}$\\
Delhi & India & 1{,}970 & $x_{(9)}$\\
Shanghai & China & 1{,}815 & $x_{(8)}$ $\leftarrow M$\\
$\vdots$ & & $\vdots$ & $\vdots$\\
Karachi & Pakistan & 1{,}430 & $x_{(1)}$\\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

**Median** ($n=15$ odd): $M = x_{(8)} = 1{,}815$.

## Quartiles and $F$-Spread

**Depth of the fourth:**
$$
\text{depth of fourth} = \frac{[\text{depth of median}] + 1}{2} = \frac{8+1}{2} = 4.5
$$

So the **fourths** (quartiles) are:
$$
F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\{x_{(4)} + x_{(5)}\} = 1{,}610, \qquad
F_U = \tfrac{1}{2}\{x_{(11)} + x_{(12)}\} = 2{,}105.
$$

**$F$-spread** and **outside bars** (outlier thresholds):
\begin{align*}
d_F &= F_U - F_L = 495\\
F_L - 1.5\,d_F &= 1{,}610 - 742.5 = 867.5\\
F_U + 1.5\,d_F &= 2{,}105 + 742.5 = 2{,}847.5
\end{align*}

$\Rightarrow$ **Tokyo** (3,420) is beyond the upper outside bar $\Rightarrow$ **outlier**.

**Five Number Summary:** $\{1{,}430;\; 1{,}610;\; 1{,}815;\; 2{,}105;\; 3{,}420\}$. \quad Mean: $\bar{x} = 1{,}939.7$.

## Constructing the Boxplot

**Algorithm:**

1. Draw a **box** with borders at $F_L$ and $F_U$ (contains 50\% of the data).
2. Draw the **median** as a solid line $|$ and the **mean** as a dotted line $\vdots$.
3. Draw **whiskers** from each box end to the most remote point that is *not* an outlier.
4. Mark outliers as $\star$ (outside $F_{UL} \pm 1.5\,d_F$) or $\bullet$ (outside $F_{UL} \pm 3\,d_F$).

## Constructing the Boxplot

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[page=2,width=0.62\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
    {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
  \caption{Boxplot for world cities. One outlier (Tokyo) is visible; the data are right-skewed.}
\end{figure}
```

## Comparing Car Fuel Economies

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=3,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
:::
::: {.column width="52%"}
**Five Number Summaries:**

\footnotesize
\begin{tabular}{lccccc}
\toprule
Group & Min & $F_L$ & $M$ & $F_U$ & Max\\
\midrule
US & 12 & 16.8 & 18.8 & 22 & 30\\
Japan & 18 & 22 & 25 & 30.5 & 35\\
EU & 14 & 19 & 23 & 25 & 28\\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}

\normalsize

**Conclusions:**

- Japanese cars are most fuel-efficient.
- One EU outlier: VW Rabbit Golf Diesel.
- US box lies entirely below Japanese box.
- Worst Japanese car outperforms $\approx 50\%$ of US cars.
:::
::::::::::::::

## Swiss Bank Notes: $X_6$ (Diagonal)

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=4,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Genuine notes** ($n=100$):

\footnotesize
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline
\# & 100 & \multicolumn{2}{l|}{Genuine}\\
\hline
$M$ & 50.5 & \multicolumn{2}{l|}{141.5}\\
\hline
$F$ & 25.75 & 141.25 & 141.8\\
\hline
& 1 & 140.65 & 142.4\\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\vspace{0.5em}
**Counterfeit notes** ($n=100$):

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline
\# & 100 & \multicolumn{2}{l|}{Counterfeit}\\
\hline
$M$ & 50.5 & \multicolumn{2}{l|}{139.5}\\
\hline
$F$ & 25.75 & 139.2 & 139.8\\
\hline
& 1 & 138.3 & 140.65\\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\normalsize

\vspace{0.3em}
$\Rightarrow$ Genuine diagonals are **systematically larger**, but with some overlap.
:::
::::::::::::::

## Swiss Bank Notes: $X_1$ (Length)

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=5,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
\vspace{1em}

- Length ($X_1$) shows **less clear separation** than diagonal ($X_6$).
- Distributions largely overlap.
- A few outliers in each group.
- Boxplots alone do **not reveal multimodality**.
:::
::::::::::::::

## Boxplot Summary

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4}
\begin{tabular}{|cp{0.82\textwidth}|}
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{\textbf{Summary: Boxplots}}\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Median and mean are measures of \textbf{location}.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Position of median/mean within the box measures \textbf{skewness}.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Box length and whisker length measure \textbf{spread} and \textbf{tail length}.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Outliers: $\star$ outside $F_{UL} \pm 1.5d_F$; $\bullet$ outside $F_{UL} \pm 3d_F$.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Boxplots do \textbf{not} reveal multimodality or clusters.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Side-by-side boxplots compare entire \textbf{distributions}.\\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

# Section 2: Histograms

## Definition of the Histogram

**Bin** of length $h$ with origin $x_0$:
$$
B_j(x_0,h) = [x_0+(j-1)h,\; x_0+jh), \quad j\in\mathbb{Z}.
$$

**Histogram estimator** for an i.i.d.\ sample $\{x_i\}_{i=1}^n$:
$$
\hat{f}_h(x) = \frac{1}{nh}
\sum_{j\in\mathbb{Z}}\sum_{i=1}^n
\indc\!\{x_i\in B_j(x_0,h)\}\,\indc\!\{x\in B_j(x_0,h)\}.
$$

**Key parameter:** binwidth $h$.

- $h$ too **large** $\Rightarrow$ over-smoothed, unstructured histogram.
- $h$ too **small** $\Rightarrow$ noisy histogram with many spurious peaks.

**Optimal binwidth** (rule of thumb): $h_{\text{opt}} = \left(\frac{24\sqrt{\pi}}{n}\right)^{1/3}.$

## Effect of Binwidth $h$

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[page=6,width=0.80\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
    {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
  \caption{Diagonal of counterfeit bank notes. Histograms with $x_0=137.8$ and
  $h=0.1$ (upper left), $h=0.2$ (lower left), $h=0.3$ (upper right), $h=0.4$ (lower right).
  }
\end{figure}
```

## Effect of Bin Origin $x_0$

**Problem:** Even with fixed $h$, different origins $x_0$ yield different histograms!

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[page=7,width=0.78\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
    {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
  \caption{Diagonal of counterfeit notes. Fixed $h=0.4$; origins $x_0\in\{137.65,137.75,137.85,137.95\}$.
  }
\end{figure}
```

**Remedy (Scott, 1985):** Average the shifted histograms!

## Averaged Shifted Histograms (ASH)

Averaging over multiple histogram origins removes the dependence on $x_0$ and reveals the **bimodal** structure of the diagonal variable.

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[page=8,width=0.78\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
    {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
  \caption{ASH based on all Swiss bank notes: 2 shifts (upper left), 4 shifts (lower left),
  8 shifts (upper right), 16 shifts (lower right). }
\end{figure}
```

## Histogram Summary

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4}
\begin{tabular}{|cp{0.82\textwidth}|}
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{\textbf{Summary: Histograms}}\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Histograms detect \textbf{modes}; modes correspond to strong peaks.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Same $h$, different $x_0$ $\Rightarrow$ different histograms.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Changing $x_0$ can drastically alter appearance.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & $h$ too large $\Rightarrow$ flat, unstructured; $h$ too small $\Rightarrow$ unstable.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Optimal $h_{\text{opt}} = (24\sqrt{\pi}/n)^{1/3}$ (under normality).\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Averaged shifted histograms are kernel density estimates.\\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

# Section 3: Kernel Densities

## From Histogram to Kernel Density

**Four critiques of histograms:**

1. Binwidth $h$ is hard to choose optimally.
2. Origin $x_0$ also influences the shape.
3. Information is lost (observations replaced by bin centre).
4. The underlying density is smooth, but the histogram is not.

**Key insight:** The histogram at a bin centre can be written as:
$$
\hat{f}_h(x) = \frac{1}{nh}\sum_{i=1}^n K\!\left(\frac{x-x_i}{h}\right),
\quad K(u) = \indc\!\left(|u|\leq\tfrac{1}{2}\right).
$$

Replacing the **uniform kernel** $K$ with a smooth function resolves difficulties (2)–(4).

## Common Kernel Functions

\begin{table}
\centering
\begin{tabular}{ll}
\toprule
$K(\bullet)$ & Name\\
\midrule
$K(u) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\indc(|u|\leqslant 1)$ & Uniform\\
$K(u) = (1-|u|)\,\indc(|u|\leqslant 1)$ & Triangle\\
$K(u) = \tfrac{3}{4}(1-u^2)\,\indc(|u|\leqslant 1)$ & Epanechnikov\\
$K(u) = \tfrac{15}{16}(1-u^2)^2\,\indc(|u|\leqslant 1)$ & Quartic (Biweight)\\
$K(u) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right)$ & Gaussian $\varphi$\\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

**Bandwidth selection** (Silverman's rule of thumb for Gaussian kernel):
$$
h_G = 1.06\,\hat{\sigma}\,n^{-1/5}, \qquad
\hat{\sigma} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n(x_i-\bar{x})^2}.
$$
For the quartic kernel: $h_Q = 2.62\cdot h_G$.

## KDE of Bank Note Diagonals

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="53%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=9,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
:::
::: {.column width="47%"}
- **Black (solid):** Genuine notes ($X_6 \approx 141.5$).
- **Red (dotted):** Counterfeit notes ($X_6 \approx 139.5$).
- Clear separation, but **overlap** near $\approx 140.4$.
- Cannot perfectly discriminate using $X_6$ alone.
- Need additional variables: $X_4$, $X_5$, \dots
:::
::::::::::::::

## Bivariate and Trivariate Density Estimates

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=10,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small 2D: $X_5$ vs $X_6$ 
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=11,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small 3D: $X_4,X_5,X_6$
:::
::::::::::::::

Two separate density modes are visible in both cases, though they still overlap. In Chapter 14 we will develop a formal **discrimination rule** to separate them.

## KDE Summary

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4}
\begin{tabular}{|cp{0.82\textwidth}|}
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{\textbf{Summary: Kernel Density Estimates}}\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & KDEs estimate densities by centring a smooth kernel on each observation.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Bandwidth $h$ controls smoothness of $\hat{f}$.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & KDEs are smooth and can represent distributions up to 3D.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Rule of thumb: $h_G = 1.06\hat{\sigma}n^{-1/5}$ (Gaussian kernel only).\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Good for detecting modes, location, skewness, tails, asymmetry.\\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

# Section 4: Scatterplots

## 2D Scatterplot: $X_5$ vs $X_6$

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=12,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
- $\circ$ = genuine notes; $*$ = counterfeit notes.
- **Downward-sloping scatter**: larger $X_5$ $\Rightarrow$ smaller $X_6$.
- The **70th observation** (thick circle) is a genuine note embedded in the counterfeit cloud.
- A horizontal separation line would misclassify it.
- Better separation requires **more variables**.
:::
::::::::::::::

## 3D Scatterplot: $X_4$, $X_5$, $X_6$

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=13,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
- Adding $X_4$ (lower inner frame distance) gives **better cluster separation**.
- The separation surface is now a **plane**, not a line:
$$
a_1 x_1 + \cdots + a_6 x_6 = \text{const}.
$$
- The optimal weights $(a_1,\dots,a_6)$ are found by **discriminant analysis** (Chapter 14).
:::
::::::::::::::

## Draftman's Plot (Scatterplot Matrix)

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[page=14,width=0.72\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
    {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
  \caption{Draftman's plot: $X_3, X_4, X_5, X_6$ of the bank data.
  Upper right half shows density contour plots. }
\end{figure}
```

- Most obvious separation: **$X_5$ vs $X_6$** (lower right).
- **Brushing**: move a resizable rectangle across one scatter; the corresponding observations are highlighted in all other scatters simultaneously $\Rightarrow$ study **conditional dependence**.

## Scatterplot Summary

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4}
\begin{tabular}{|cp{0.82\textwidth}|}
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{\textbf{Summary: Scatterplots}}\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & 2D/3D scatterplots identify separated points, outliers, sub-clusters.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Slope of scatter indicates positive/negative dependence.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Draftman plot matrices detect structures conditioned on other variables.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Brushing through a point cloud reveals conditional dependence.\\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

# Section 5: Chernoff–Flury Faces

## The Idea of Faces

**Principle:** Map each $p$-dimensional observation onto characteristics of a human face, exploiting the brain's powerful face-recognition ability.

- Introduced by **Chernoff (1973)**; extended by **Flury \& Riedwyl (1988)**.
- Each variable is rescaled to $[0,1]$; the two extremes correspond to extreme face element positions.
- Flury–Riedwyl design: **36 face elements** (18 for left side, 18 for right side).

**Bank data assignment** ($p=6$):

| Variable | Face Element |
|:---------|:-------------|
| $X_1$ | Eye sizes (1, 19) |
| $X_2$ | Pupil sizes (2, 20) |
| $X_3$ | Eye slants (4, 22) |
| $X_4$ | Upper hair lines (11, 29) |
| $X_5$ | Lower hair lines (12, 30) |
| $X_6$ | Face lines + darkness of hair (13, 14, 31, 32) |

## Faces for Observations 91–110

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[page=15,width=0.80\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
    {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
  \caption{Chernoff–Flury faces for observations 91--110 of the bank data.
  Observations 91--100: \textbf{counterfeit} (upper); 101--110: \textbf{genuine} (lower).
  }
\end{figure}
```

## Faces for All Genuine Notes (Obs.\ 1–100)

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=16,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small Obs.\ 1–50 
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=17,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small Obs.\ 51–100 
:::
::::::::::::::

Genuine notes: **darker appearance**, **broader face lines** (reflecting larger diagonal $X_6$).

## Faces for All Counterfeit Notes (Obs.\ 101–200)

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=18,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small Obs.\ 101–150 
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=19,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small Obs.\ 151–200
:::
::::::::::::::

Counterfeit notes: **lighter hair**, **narrower face lines** — clearly different from genuine group.

## Faces Summary

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4}
\begin{tabular}{|cp{0.82\textwidth}|}
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{\textbf{Summary: Chernoff–Flury Faces}}\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Sub-groups are characterised by \textbf{similar-looking faces}.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Outliers appear as \textbf{extreme faces} (e.g.\ dark hair, big smile).\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & If one element of $X$ is unusual, the corresponding face element changes dramatically.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Variable assignment to face elements matters for interpretability.\\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

# Section 6: Andrews' Curves

## Definition

**Idea (Andrews, 1972):** Map each $p$-dimensional observation $X_i$ to a Fourier curve:
$$
f_i(t) = \begin{cases}
\dfrac{X_{i,1}}{\sqrt{2}} + X_{i,2}\sin t + X_{i,3}\cos t + \cdots
+ X_{i,p}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{p}{2}t\right) & p \text{ even}\\[1ex]
\dfrac{X_{i,1}}{\sqrt{2}} + X_{i,2}\sin t + X_{i,3}\cos t + \cdots
+ X_{i,p}\cos\!\left(\tfrac{p-1}{2}t\right) & p \text{ odd}
\end{cases}
$$
where $t \in [-\pi, \pi]$.

**Example** ($p=3$): $X_1=(0,0,1)$, $X_2=(1,0,0)$, $X_3=(0,1,0)$ give
$$
f_1(t) = \cos t, \quad f_2(t) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad f_3(t) = \sin t. \quad \text{(Distinct!)}
$$

**Bank note example** (obs.\ 96, order $1,2,\dots,6$):
$$
f_{96}(t) = \tfrac{215.6}{\sqrt{2}} + 129.9\sin t + 129.9\cos t + 9.0\sin 2t + 9.5\cos 2t + 141.7\sin 3t.
$$

## Andrews' Curves: Variable Order Matters

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=20,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small Order: $1,2,3,4,5,6$
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=21,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small Order: $6,5,4,3,2,1$
:::
::::::::::::::

\vspace{0.3em}

- **Black:** genuine (obs.\ 96–100); **Red dotted:** counterfeit (obs.\ 101–105).
- Original order: differences appear in **high frequency** (hard to see).
- Reversed order: differences shift to **intercept** level.
- **Andrews' recommendation:** use order suggested by **Principal Component Analysis** (Chapter 11).

## Andrews' Curves Summary

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4}
\begin{tabular}{|cp{0.82\textwidth}|}
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{\textbf{Summary: Andrews' Curves}}\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Outliers appear as single curves \textbf{deviating from the bundle}.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Sub-groups are characterised by \textbf{sets of similar curves}.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & The \textbf{order of variables} strongly affects interpretation.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Optimal ordering is provided by \textbf{PCA} (Chapter 11).\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & For $>20$ observations: bad signal-to-ink ratio (Tufte, 1983).\\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

# Section 7: Parallel Coordinates Plots

## PCP: Concept and Construction

**Parallel Coordinates Plot (PCP)** (Inselberg, 1985):

- Draw $p$ parallel vertical axes (one per variable).
- Scale all variables to $[\min, \max] = [0, 1]$.
- Represent each observation $x_i$ as a **polyline** connecting its scaled values on the $p$ axes.

**Advantages over Cartesian plots:**

- Works for $p > 4$ (no dimensionality barrier).
- Positive dependence: lines approximately **parallel**.
- Negative dependence: lines **intersect** near the midpoint.
- Discrete variables and sub-groups are **visible**.

## PCP for Bank Note Observations 96–105

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[page=22,width=0.82\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
    {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
  \caption{PCP of observations 96--105 (genuine: black solid; counterfeit: red dash-dot).
  }
\end{figure}
```

## Detecting Linear Dependence

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=23,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small Weight vs Displacement ($\rho=0.9$) 
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=24,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small Mileage vs Weight ($\rho=-0.82$)
:::
::::::::::::::

- **Near-parallel lines** $\Rightarrow$ strong **positive** correlation.
- **Lines crossing near midpoint** $\Rightarrow$ strong **negative** correlation.

## PCP for Sub-Group Detection and Outliers

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=25,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small Displacement, Gear Ratio, Headquarters
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=26,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small Headroom, Rear Seat, Trunk Space
:::
::::::::::::::

- **Left:** Convergence to 3 discrete headquarters values (USA / Japan / EU).
- **Right:** Two **outliers** clearly visible — confirmed by the boxplot below.

## Boxplot Confirmation and Cubic Spline PCP

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=27,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=29,width=0.9\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small Cubic spline PCP 
:::
::::::::::::::

- **Crossing ambiguity** in standard PCP (obs.\ $A$ and $B$ sharing a value at $j=2$): unresolvable with straight lines.
- **Cubic spline** interpolation resolves which curve belongs to which observation.

## Full Car Data PCP with Colouring

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[page=30,width=0.80\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
    {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
  \caption{PCP for all 13 car data variables, coloured by headquarters:
  \textcolor{red}{red} = EU, \textcolor{blue}{blue} = Japan, black = US.}
\end{figure}
```

**Findings:** US cars (black): large $X_7$–$X_{11}$ (size/weight); Japanese cars (blue): high $X_3, X_4$ (repair record).

## PCP Summary

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4}
\begin{tabular}{|cp{0.82\textwidth}|}
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{\textbf{Summary: Parallel Coordinates Plots}}\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & PCP overcomes the Cartesian limitation for dimensions $>4$.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Outliers appear as \textbf{outlying polygon curves}.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Near-parallel lines indicate \textbf{positive} dependence; crossing lines indicate \textbf{negative} dependence.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Variable ordering matters; sub-groups may be revealed by selective \textbf{colouring}.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Cubic spline interpolation resolves crossing ambiguity.\\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

# Section 8: Hexagon Plots

## Motivation and Algorithm

**Problem:** For large $n$, ordinary scatterplots suffer from **overplotting** (many points on top of each other).

**Hexagon binning** (Lewin-Koh, 2006):

1. Tessellate the $(x,y)$-plane with a regular grid of hexagons.
2. Count the number of points $n_k$ in each hexagon $k$.
3. Colour or scale hexagons in proportion to $n_k$.

**Advantages of hexagons over squares:**

- **Symmetry of nearest neighbours** (equidistant centres).
- Maximum number of sides for a regular plane tessellation.
- Less visually biased for displaying densities.
- Extremely fast — works for $n \geqslant 10^6$.

## Hexagon Plots: Age vs Income and Age vs Computer Time

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=31,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small $X_1$ (Age) vs $X_2$ (Net income)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=32,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small $X_1$ (Age) vs $X_5$ (Computer time)
:::
::::::::::::::

ALLBUS 2006 survey data ($n=2{,}946$). Most respondents earn $<2{,}000$ EUR/month; $\approx 40\%$ of all age groups use a computer $<1$ h/week.

## Hexagon Plot: Income vs Flat Size

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[page=33,width=0.62\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
    {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
  \caption{Hexagon plot: $X_2$ (Net income) vs $X_7$ (Flat size).}
\end{figure}
```

**Clear positive trend:** Higher net income $\Rightarrow$ larger flat. A few high-income respondents live in small flats.

## Hexagon Plot Summary

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4}
\begin{tabular}{|cp{0.82\textwidth}|}
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{\textbf{Summary: Hexagon Plots}}\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Hexagon binning is a \textbf{bivariate histogram} for large data.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Variance and bias trade off in opposite directions with bin width.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Hexagons have the property of \textbf{symmetry of nearest neighbours}.\\
\hline
$\hookrightarrow$ & Hexagons are visually \textbf{less biased} for density display than squares.\\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

# Section 9: Boston Housing

## Dataset Overview

**Boston Housing** (Harrison \& Rubinfeld, 1978):

- **Goal:** Does "clean air" influence house prices?
- $n = 506$ census tracts in the Boston metropolitan area.
- 14 variables $X_1, \dots, X_{14}$ (see Chapter 22 for full description).
- $X_{14}$: median value of owner-occupied homes (target).

**Key variables:**
$X_1$ crime rate, $X_3$ non-retail business, $X_5$ nitric oxide concentration, $X_6$ rooms/dwelling, $X_{11}$ pupil/teacher ratio, $X_{13}$ lower-status population, $X_{14}$ median home value.

## PCP for Boston Housing

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[page=34,width=0.80\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
    {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
  \caption{PCP of Boston housing data. Observations with $X_{14} > \text{median}(X_{14})$
  are coloured \textcolor{red}{red}.}
\end{figure}
```

- Strong **negative** dependence: $X_{13}$ and $X_{14}$.
- Red lines dominate the **upper** part of $X_{12}$, **lower** part of $X_{11}$.
- $X_1$ and $X_9$: values **concentrated near 0** $\Rightarrow$ transformations needed.

## Scatterplot Matrices (Original and Transformed)

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=35,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small $X_1,\dots,X_5,X_{14}$
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=latex}
\includegraphics[page=36,width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
  {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
```
\centering\small $\tilde{X}_1,\dots,\tilde{X}_5,\tilde{X}_{14}$
:::
::::::::::::::

- **Right:** Transformed data show more linear relationships.
- Black = cheaper districts; \textcolor{red}{red} = expensive districts.

## Proposed Transformations

To reduce asymmetry in the marginal distributions:

\begin{align*}
\tilde{X}_1 &= \log X_1, & \tilde{X}_2 &= X_2/10, & \tilde{X}_3 &= \log X_3,\\
\tilde{X}_5 &= \log X_5, & \tilde{X}_6 &= \log X_6, & \tilde{X}_7 &= X_7^{2.5}/10000,\\
\tilde{X}_8 &= \log X_8, & \tilde{X}_9 &= \log X_9, & \tilde{X}_{10} &= \log X_{10},\\
\tilde{X}_{11} &= e^{0.4 X_{11}}/1000, & \tilde{X}_{12} &= X_{12}/100, & \tilde{X}_{13} &= \sqrt{X_{13}},\\
\tilde{X}_{14} &= \log X_{14}. & & & &
\end{align*}

Note: $X_4$ is binary — **no transformation**.

## Boxplots Before and After Transformation

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[page=37,width=0.80\linewidth,keepaspectratio]%
    {haerdle_01_figs.pdf}
  \caption{Boxplots for all variables before (top) and after (bottom) transformation.
  }
\end{figure}
```

Transformed variables are **more symmetric** and have **fewer outliers**.

## Key Variable Relationships

| Variable | Relation to $X_{14}$ | Remark |
|:---------|:---------------------|:-------|
| $X_1$ (crime) | Negative | Two sub-groups visible in $\log X_1$ |
| $X_3$ (non-retail) | Negative | Bimodal KDE |
| $X_5$ (NOx) | Negative | May proxy noise pollution |
| $X_6$ (rooms) | **Positive** | Strongest linear signal |
| $X_{11}$ (pupil/teacher) | Negative | Fewer teachers $\Rightarrow$ lower prices |
| $X_{13}$ (lower status) | **Strongest negative** | Nearly linear on log scale |

# Exercises

## Selected Exercises

\textbf{Exercise 1.3} Assume $X \sim N(0,1)$. What percentage of data falls outside the outside bars $[F_L - 1.5d_F,\; F_U + 1.5d_F]$?

\textbf{Exercise 1.9} Use a bandwidth selection criterion to find the optimal $h$ for the diagonal variable of the bank notes. Is one bandwidth for both groups better?

\textbf{Exercise 1.10} The two density groups overlap near diagonal $\approx 140.4$. Can we effectively separate them using $X_6$ alone?

\textbf{Exercise 1.15} Heights of 13 students:
$$1.72,\ 1.83,\ 1.74,\ 1.79,\ 1.94,\ 1.81,\ 1.66,\ 1.60,\ 1.78,\ 1.77,\ 1.85,\ 1.70,\ 1.76.$$
(1) Five-number summary. \quad (2) Boxplot. \quad (3) Histogram.

\textbf{Exercise 1.18} Draftman plot for car data ($X_1 = \text{price}$, $X_2 = \text{mileage}$, $X_8 = \text{weight}$, $X_9 = \text{length}$). Brush on heavy cars. What can you say about price, mileage, length?

## Chapter Summary

**Methods covered in Chapter 1:**

| Method | Detects | Limitation |
|:-------|:--------|:-----------|
| Boxplot | Location, spread, outliers, skew | No multimodality |
| Histogram | Modes, distribution shape | Sensitive to $h$ and $x_0$ |
| KDE | Modes, shape (smooth) | Bandwidth choice |
| Scatterplot | Bivariate/trivariate dependence | Max 3D |
| Faces | Cluster structure, outliers | Variable assignment |
| Andrews' curves | Clusters, outliers (curves) | Variable order |
| PCP | High-dim.\ structure, clusters | Crossing ambiguity |
| Hexagon plot | Large data density | Bin width choice |

**Recurring dataset:** Swiss bank notes — genuine vs.\ counterfeit ($n=200$, $p=6$).

**Next:** Chapter 2 introduces formal multivariate statistics: mean vectors, covariance matrices, and sample geometry.
